name: f(R) modified gravity
summary: Replaces the Einstein-Hilbert action R with a general function f(R). Introduces an effective scalar degree of freedom (the 'scalaron') which can drive cosmic acceleration without a true cosmological constant. Specific functional forms (Hu-Sawicki, Starobinsky, Tsujikawa) reproduce LCDM at high redshift while modifying late-time dynamics. Cosmic-acceleration-driving f(R) is constrained by solar-system tests (chameleon screening) and by structure-growth measurements.

addresses_puzzles.note: Replaces Lambda with the dynamics of the scalaron. Doesn't resolve the underlying smallness of the late-time acceleration scale; trades one fine-tuning for another. Late-time-acceleration variants are tightly constrained by RSD and ISW; LZ-friendly variants are even more restricted.
Starobinsky inflation (R + R^2/M^2) is a special case of f(R) gravity that reproduces Planck observations excellently — one of the leading inflation models.

touches_benchmarks.note: Solar-system tests bound the scalaron-mediated fifth force; chameleon-screened f(R) variants survive. PPN gamma constraints from Cassini Shapiro delay restrict |f''(R)/f'(R)| at galactic scales.
Pure f(R) gravity does not change tensor mode propagation speed; consistent with c_GW = c.
f(R) modifies gravitational growth of structure; constraint sigma_8(z) from RSD constrains f(R) dark-energy-driving variants.

new_fields: effective scalaron from f(R) -> f'(R) Einstein-frame mapping
new_scales: scalaron mass M ~ 10^-30 - 10^13 GeV depending on use case
introduces.note: Functional shape of f(R); typical late-DE models have 2-3 parameters (Hu-Sawicki uses 3).

history: Buchdahl (1970) introduced general f(R) gravity. Starobinsky (1980) used R + R^2 for inflation. Modern late-time-acceleration f(R) (Hu-Sawicki 2007, Starobinsky 2007, Tsujikawa 2008). Comprehensive review: Sotiriou and Faraoni (2010), 'f(R) theories of gravity', Rev. Mod. Phys. 82, 451, arXiv:0805.1726.

references.citation: Sotiriou and Faraoni, 'f(R) theories of gravity', Rev. Mod. Phys. 82, 451 (2010)
